run.output:
Q3: 10 rows, 615.26 ms
User time: 6.23s
System time: 0.55s
CPU utilization: 985%
Max RSS: 604100 KB

validation.comparison_result: Q3 results match ground truth: 10 rows expected, 10 rows actual

Q3.notes: Query executes correctly with parallel processing (985% CPU), but 5-8x slower than DuckDB (78-113ms)

Q3.bottleneck: Zone maps loaded but never used to skip blocks; hash table probes on large qualified_orders map (3M entries, ~50MB) cause cache thrashing; suboptimal madvise hints for random access patterns
Q3.root_cause: Implementation scans 100% of orders and lineitem blocks despite having zone maps that could skip ~50% of blocks. The qualified_orders hash table (3M entries after filtering) exceeds L3 cache (44MB), causing cache misses on every probe during the 60M lineitem scan. Additionally, MADV_SEQUENTIAL hint is wrong for join key columns that are accessed randomly during hash probes.

analysis.overall: Q3 runs correctly in 615ms but is 5-8x slower than DuckDB's 78-113ms. The implementation has good parallel structure (985% CPU) and loads zone maps, but NEVER uses them to skip blocks. This is the biggest missed optimization — both o_orderdate and l_shipdate predicates are range filters on zonemap-indexed columns with ~50% selectivity, so we could skip ~50% of blocks in both tables. Secondary bottleneck is hash table cache thrashing during lineitem probe phase.

performance_optimizations.description: The code loads zone maps for o_orderdate and l_shipdate (lines 256, 316) but never uses them. Add zone map consultation in scan_orders and scan_lineitem to skip entire blocks (100K rows each) whose min/max don't satisfy the predicates. For orders: skip blocks where max_orderdate >= 1995-03-15. For lineitem: skip blocks where min_shipdate <= 1995-03-15. Implementation: Before processing each morsel, check if the morsel's start_idx falls in a skippable zone by iterating through orders_zonemap/lineitem_zonemap and comparing zone boundaries. This requires converting morsel boundaries to zone indices (zone_idx = start_idx / 100000) and checking the min/max values. Expected to skip ~50% of orders blocks and ~50% of lineitem blocks, reducing I/O and CPU by ~40-50% overall.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 40-50% reduction in execution time (615ms → 310-370ms). This brings us within 3-4x of DuckDB. Zone map skipping is the single highest-impact optimization for range predicates on zonemap-indexed columns. At SF10, orders has ~150 zones, lineitem has ~600 zones — checking these is trivial compared to scanning 100K rows per zone.

performance_optimizations.description: Build a Bloom filter on qualified_orders keys (~3M entries) during Step 2, then use it in Step 3 to skip lineitem rows whose l_orderkey is definitely not in qualified_orders before probing the hash table. This reduces expensive hash table probes (random memory access) by ~80-90% for non-matching keys. Implementation: After building qualified_orders, create a std::vector<uint64_t> bloom filter (size: 3M * 10 bits / 64 = ~469KB, fits in L2 cache), hash each qualified order key into the filter. In scan_lineitem, check the bloom filter before probing qualified_orders. Use 2 hash functions for ~1% false positive rate. Reference: knowledge/indexing/bloom-filters.md and knowledge/joins/hash-join-variants.md line 15.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-25% speedup (reduces remaining time after zone maps by 15-25%, so 310-370ms → 230-315ms). Bloom filter is tiny (469KB), fits entirely in L2 cache (32MB), so lookups are very fast. Since most lineitem rows don't join (PK-FK with filtered orders), we avoid ~80% of expensive qualified_orders probes.

performance_optimizations.description: Currently all mmapped columns use MADV_SEQUENTIAL (line 86), which is correct for scan-only columns but wrong for join key columns that are accessed via random hash lookups. Change madvise strategy: keep MADV_SEQUENTIAL for scan-only columns (c_mktsegment, o_orderdate, o_shippriority, l_shipdate, l_extendedprice, l_discount), but use MADV_RANDOM for join key columns (c_custkey, o_custkey, o_orderkey, l_orderkey) since these are accessed via hash probes in unpredictable order. Additionally, use MADV_WILLNEED for c_mktsegment (1.5MB, dictionary-encoded, 100% scan) to prefetch the entire column into cache before filtering. Reference: knowledge/storage/persistent-storage.md lines 9-10, knowledge/external-libs/io-libraries.md.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% speedup (reduces I/O stalls for random access patterns). The OS currently prefetches sequential blocks, but hash joins access data randomly, causing page faults. MADV_RANDOM tells the OS to reduce readahead, minimizing wasted I/O. MADV_WILLNEED on tiny columns eliminates page fault overhead entirely.

performance_optimizations.description: Current morsel sizes (10K for customer, 50K for orders, 100K for lineitem) are not tuned for cache behavior. Optimal morsel size = L3_per_thread / (num_active_columns * sizeof(column)). With 44MB L3, 64 threads, L3_per_thread = 688KB. For customer (2 columns, 5 bytes): 688KB / 10B = ~69K rows. For orders (4 columns, 16 bytes): 688KB / 16B = ~43K rows. For lineitem (4 columns, 20 bytes): 688KB / 20B = ~34K rows. Adjust morsel sizes to match these targets to maximize L3 hit rate and reduce inter-thread cache contention. Reference: knowledge/parallelism/thread-parallelism.md.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-8% speedup (better cache locality reduces memory stalls). This is a fine-tuning optimization after the major I/O and join optimizations.

performance_optimizations.description: Current AggKeyHash (lines 169-177) uses multiplicative hashing with bit shifts. For a 3-field composite key (l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority), use a stronger hash to reduce collisions. Replace with XXHash or MurmurHash-style mixing: combine all 3 fields with multiply-xor-shift sequences. Better mixing reduces hash table collisions, improving aggregation performance. Alternatively, since l_orderkey is likely unique (primary key), use it directly as the hash (no mixing needed), storing the full AggKey in the hash table value. Reference: knowledge/data-structures/compact-hash-tables.md, knowledge/aggregation/hash-aggregation.md.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-5% speedup (reduces hash collisions in aggregation phase). The aggregation map has ~300K distinct keys, so collision reduction matters for probe performance.

performance_optimizations.description: Step 2 builds qualified_orders hash table by sequentially merging per-thread maps (lines 293-299). With 64 threads, this merge can be parallelized: partition the final hash table by hash(o_orderkey) % 64, have each thread merge its local map into the corresponding partition. This eliminates the sequential merge bottleneck and reduces memory contention. Implementation: Use std::vector<std::unordered_map<int32_t, OrderInfo>> for the partitioned global table, merge in parallel. Reference: knowledge/parallelism/data-partitioning.md, knowledge/joins/hash-join-variants.md (radix partitioning).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-7% speedup (parallelizes the sequential merge phase). The merge currently processes ~3M entries sequentially, taking ~20-30ms. Parallelizing across 64 threads reduces this to <5ms.

summary: Q3 iteration 1 executes correctly in 615ms (10 rows, matches ground truth) but is 5-8x slower than DuckDB (78-113ms). The implementation uses parallel processing effectively (985% CPU utilization) and has good structure, but misses the single most important optimization: ZONE MAP BLOCK SKIPPING. Zone maps are loaded but never consulted, causing 100% of blocks to be scanned despite having range predicates with ~50% selectivity on zonemap-indexed columns. This is the #1 bottleneck. Secondary bottlenecks are hash table cache thrashing (qualified_orders exceeds L3), wrong madvise hints (SEQUENTIAL instead of RANDOM for join keys), and suboptimal morsel sizes. Recommended optimizations are listed in priority order, with zone map skipping expected to provide 40-50% speedup alone, bringing execution time to 310-370ms. Combined with bloom filter and other optimizations, we should reach 200-250ms (within 2-3x of DuckDB).
